coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposites on carbon cloth as a binder-free electrode for supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Hong, Yiwen
Xu, Jingli
Chung, Jin Suk
Choi, Won Mook
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/Ni(OH)2 composites on carbon cloth (G-NH//CC) are prepared through simple hydrothermal reactions. The resulting G-NH//CC is employed as a binder-free electrode of supercapacitors. Due to the enhanced electrical conductivity and efficient ion transport by the addition of GQDs, the G-NH//CC electrode exhibits enhanced electrochemical performances. Specifically, the G-NH//CC delivers a maximum specific capacitance of 1825 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 as well as a good cycle stability of 83.5 % capacity retention after 8000 cycles. Additionally, all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor (SSC) is assembled with G-NH//CC composites as both positive and negative electrodes. The fabricated SSC exhibits a high energy density of 80.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 2021 W kg−1. The present study provides a facile and efficient strategy to prepare high-performance electrode materials for advanced electrochemical energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Ni(OH)2
Carbon clothes
Symmetric supercapacitors

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst pubtype ref

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by The Ministry of Education ( NRF-2018R1D1A1B07047042 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of GQDs/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposite on carbon cloth.
(a–c) SEM images of G-NH//CC nanocomposite. (d) TEM image of G-NH//CC and (e) its high magnification image. (f) EDS elementary mapping of G-NH//CC.
(a) The XPS survey scan of G10-NH//CC and NH//CC. (b) High resolution C 1s and (c) Ni 2p XPS spectrum of G10-NH//CC.
(a) CV curves of different G-NH//CC composites and NH//CC. (b) CV curves of G10-NH//CC at different scan rates.

(a) Galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of different G-NH//CC composites and NH//CC, (b) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of G10-NH//CC at different current densities. (c) Obtained specific capacitance of G10-NH//CC at different current densities. (d) Nyquist plots of G10-NH//CC and NH//CC.
(a) Specific capacitance retention of G10-NH//CC and NH//CC. (b) Nyquist plots of G10-NH//CC and NH//CC after 2000 cycles.
Simplified energy band diagram of GQDs and Ni(OH)2 (a) before contact and (b) after contact.

(a) Schematic illustration of all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor using G-NH//CC electrodes and photo images of the bending state. (b) CV curves at different scan rates and (c) galvanostatic charge-discharge curves at different current densities of the G-NH//CC SSC. (d) The Ragone plots of the G-NH//CC SSC and reported Ni(OH)2-based supercapacitors. (e) CV curves of the G-NH//CC SSC at different bending states. (f) Photo image of lighting LED bulb powered by two G-NH//CC SSCs in series.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposites on carbon cloth as a binder-free electrode for supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44610, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, 93 Daehak-ro Nam-gu, Ulsan 44610, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science, LongTeng Road 333, 201620, Shanghai, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanghai University of Engineering Science, LongTeng Road 333, 201620 Shanghai, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/Ni(OH)2 composites on carbon cloth (G-NH//CC) are prepared through simple hydrothermal reactions. The resulting G-NH//CC is employed as a binder-free electrode of supercapacitors. Due to the enhanced electrical conductivity and efficient ion transport by the addition of GQDs, the G-NH//CC electrode exhibits enhanced electrochemical performances. Specifically, the G-NH//CC delivers a maximum specific capacitance of 1825 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 as well as a good cycle stability of 83.5 % capacity retention after 8000 cycles. Additionally, all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor (SSC) is assembled with G-NH//CC composites as both positive and negative electrodes. The fabricated SSC exhibits a high energy density of 80.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 2021 W kg−1. The present study provides a facile and efficient strategy to prepare high-performance electrode materials for advanced electrochemical energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: In response to the large increase in energy demands within various areas of transportation and portable electronic devices, supercapacitors have attracted considerable attention due to their higher power density, fast charging time, long cycle life, and excellent stability [1–3]. However, the low energy density of supercapacitors is still one important area for improvement, especially for electrical double layer capacitors (EDLC) which use carbon-based active materials (activated carbon, graphene, carbon nanotubes, etc.). Pseudocapacitors, which mainly utilize the fast and reversible surface Faradic redox reaction of transition metal oxide and hydroxide materials such as MnO2 [4,5], NiO [6], Ni(OH)2 [7–11], and Co3O4 [12], can deliver a substantially higher specific capacitance [13,14]. Among these metal oxide-based materials, Ni(OH)2 has received significant attention for its high theoretical specific capacitance, its high stability in alkaline electrolyte, its abundance, and environmentally friendly [9,8–11]. However, the actual specific capacitance of Ni(OH)2-based supercapacitor highly depends on its nanostructures and crystal structure, and the poor electrical conductivity (∼10−17 S cm−1) Ni(OH)2 is significantly lower than its theoretical value [15]. To solve these drawbacks, various research has proposed the use of nanocomposites of Ni(OH)2 with high electrical conductive carbon-based materials such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, and activated carbon [16–18]. However, the incorporation of conductive carbon materials only represents a limited performance enhancement, since carbon materials on the surface of Ni(OH)2 could disturb the redox reaction between Ni(OH)2 and the electrolyte, as well as reducing the specific capacitance of nanocomposite due to their small capacitances.

In this regard, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new class of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterials, could be considered as an attractive material due to their small size (< 10 nm), high conductivity, excellent stability, and easy control of surface functional groups. Thus, GQDs could be employed as electrodes for energy conversion and storage applications including batteries and supercapacitors due to their excellent electrochemical properties [19–23]. To enhance the electrical conductivity of the electrode, an efficient approach is taken using binder-free active materials grown on three-dimensional conductive substrates. Carbon cloth (CC), consisting of carbon fibers has been used extensively as a conductive substrate in supercapacitors and batteries due to its excellent flexibility, porous structure, and high conductivity. In supercapacitors, CC is employed as a current collector for the direct growth of metal oxides, because it can provide fast electron transfer and efficient diffusion of electrolytes with a simple electrode structure.

Herein, we report a simple and cost-effective synthesis process for GQDs/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposites on CC (denoted as G-NH//CC), which is directly used as binder-free electrodes in high-performance flexible supercapacitors. The G-NH//CC was prepared through simple hydrothermal synthesis of Ni(OH)2 in the presence of GQDs. The as-prepared sample shows a specific capacitance of 1825 F g−1 at a scan rate of 1 A g−1 and good cycle stability for 8000 cycles. Also, we assembled all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor using the G-NH//CC electrodes, which demonstrates the excellent performance of high energy density of 80.8 Wh·kg-1 at power density 2021 W·kg−1 with good mechanical stability. These results show that the G-NH//CC can be a promising electrode for high-performance flexible supercapacitors.

GQDs were synthesized using the carbonization of citric acid via a hydrothermal process [24]. Citric acid solution (50 mL, 1 mg/L) was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 4 h. Then, the obtained solution was then dialyzed using dialysis tubing (3000 Da, Spectrum Lab. Inc) against DI water for 1 day to remove impurities, resulting in the GQDs solution (2 mg/mL).

Prior to the hydrothermal process, the CC (HCP330, WizMAC, 160 g cm2, 320 μm-thick) was cleaned with acetone and DI water under ultrasonication for 10 min, followed by UV/ozone treatment for 4 h. Typically, the UV/ozone treated CC was immersed in a reaction solution (50 mL) containing Ni(NO3)2
∙ 6H2O (5 mmol), urea (10 mmol), and the prepared GQDs. The reaction solution was stirred at 60 °C for 2 h and then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an oven at 120 °C for 24 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and the obtained samples were washed several times in DI water and ethanol. The samples were denoted as G10-NH//CC, meaning that 10 mg of GQDs was added in the reaction solution. For comparison, Ni(OH)2 was also synthesized on CC without GQDs (denoted as NH//CC).

The microstructure of the obtained samples was investigated by means of scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6500F, JEOL) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 F, JEOL). Measurements of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements (XPS; Thermo Fisher) were performed using monochromatic AlKα radiation (hv = 1486.6 eV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Nicolet IR 200 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific). Raman spectroscopy (DXR Raman spectrometer, Thermo Scientific) was measured at room temperature with a 532 nm excitation source.

The electrochemical performance of the prepared samples was conducted in a three-electrode system with aqueous 2 M KOH electrolytes. The G-NH//CC directly served as the working electrode without a binder, a platinum wire as the counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were carried out with a multichannel potentiostat-galvanostat (VSP, Bio-logic). The potential range for CV measurements were from -0.35 to 0.65 V and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves were tested in a voltage ranging from -0.2 to 0.3 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured in a frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz with an AC perturbation of 5 mV.

The solid-state symmetric supercapacitor was fabricated by assembling two G-NH//CC as positive and negative electrodes with PVA/KOH gel electrolyte and a filter paper as a separator. The PVA/KOH gel electrolyte was papered as followings: 6 g of PVA and 3.5 g of KOH was mixed with 60 ml of DI water with continuous stirring at 90 °C until a clear solution was obtained. Then, the solution was cooled down to room temperature for the gel formation. Two G-NH//CC electrodes were dipped into the gel electrolyte for 10 min and then assembled face to face with filter paper between them using gentle pressure.

The fabrication procedure of G-NH//CC is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . First, the as-synthesized GQDs was characterized by Raman spectroscopy and FT-IR (Fig. S1). The Raman spectra of GQDs exhibits two distinct peaks of D-peak (∼1350 cm−1) and G-peak (∼1590 cm−1) and the FT-IR spectra shows characteristic peaks related to the deformation of CO (∼1380 cm−1), in-plane vibration of CC (∼1640 cm−1), the stretching vibration of CO in the carboxyl group (∼1720 cm−1), and the stretching vibration of OH (∼3460 cm−1). These results indicate the successful synthesis of GQDs by carbonization of citric acid. The pristine CC displays black color and consists of uniform carbon fibers with a smooth surface (Fig. S2). After the hydrothermal reaction, the surface morphology of the G-NH//CC shows the formation of Ni(OH)2 nanowalls vertically grown of on the surface of carbon fibers, as shown in Fig. 2 a–c. It is also found that the G-NH//CC has similar nanostructure with NH//CC, but it has more irregular bumps on the surface of carbon fibers (Fig. S3). The HRTEM image of the G-NH//CC in Fig. 2d shows that the GQDs are evenly bounded on the Ni(OH)2 surface. Additionally, the high-resolution image in Fig. 2e exhibits the lattice fringe of GQDs in the G-NH//CC sample, where the lattice spacing distance is 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1120) lattice of graphene. The SEM image and corresponding elemental mapping images by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) in Fig. 2f clearly show the uniform distribution of Ni, O, and C elements in the obtained G-NH//CC composite, confirming the successful synthesis of GQDs/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposite.

XPS measurements were carried out to analyse the chemical compositions of G-NH//CC composite. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the XPS survey spectrum of G10-NH//CC sample shows the presence of Ni, O, and C in the composite, while C peak was not observed for NH//CC. The content of carbon in G10-NH//CC composite is calculated to be 3.8 % from the XPS spectrum. The deconvolution of C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3b shows different oxygen containing carbon functional groups, including sp2 CC at 284.3 eV, sp3 CC at 285.4 eV, COC at 287.4 eV and CO at 288.5 eV. These peaks clarify the presence of GQDs in the composite sample. The deconvolution of Ni 2p spectrum of G10-NH//CC in Fig. 3c can be assigned to two major peaks with satellite peaks of Ni 2p3/2 (850–865 eV) and Ni 2p1/2 (870–885 eV) spin-orbit levels, which shows Ni ion is in +2 valence state. The observed spin energy separation between Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 peaks are 17.6 eV, indicating characteristics of Ni(OH)2 phase, and in accordance with previous reports [25].

The electrochemical performance of the obtained samples was investigated by a three-electrode system. The capacitive behaviors of the samples were measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 (Fig. 4 a). Two clear anodic and cathodic peaks are observed for all samples in the CV curves, indicating the pseudocapacitive behavior of Ni(OH)2-based electrode. This result indicates that the capacitance characteristics are mainly ascribed to the Faradic reactions. The current output in the CV curves shows that G-NH//CC composites are obviously higher than that of NH//CC at the same scan rate, indicating higher capacitance. This could be ascribed to the enhanced ion transport and electrical conductivity owing to the addition of GQDs. The specific capacitance was calculated from the CV curves using the equation below.
C s
=
∫
I
V
d V
m × v × Δ V
where
∫
I
V
d V
is the area enclosed by the CV curves, m is the mass of active material, v is the scan rate, and Δ V is the potential range. The calculated specific capacitance at 20 mV s−1 reveals that the G10-NH//CC composite shows superior performance of 1089 F g−1 compared to other samples (NH//CC = 545 F g−1, G5-NH//CC = 753 F g−1, and G15-NH//CC = 894 F g−1). Fig. 4b shows the CV curves of the G10-NH//CC composite at different scan rates. It is shown that the current density simultaneously increases with the increasing scan rate, and the CV curves with symmetric redox peaks demonstrate a high reversibility of the charge-discharge reaction. In addition, the potentials of the anodic and cathodic peaks shift to more positive and negative potentials with the increasing scan rate, respectively, which is attributed to the polarization effect and ion diffusion.

The galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of the prepared samples were measured under voltage ranging from −0.2 to 0.3 V at a current density of 1 A g−1. The charge-discharge curves in Fig. 5 a demonstrate that the area of the curves and the discharging time increase with the addition of GQDs, showing the specific capacitance is significantly enhanced due to GQDs. G10-NH//CC composite exhibits best charge-discharge performance among the prepared electrodes, which is optimal GQDs content. Also, the galvanostatic charge-discharge measurement of the G10-NH//CC composite was performed at a different current density from 1 A g−1 to 10 A g−1 (Fig. 5b). A distinct potential plateau is observed in the charge and discharge curves, and it almost disappears at the current density of 10 A g−1, showing the pseudocapacitive characteristics of Ni(OH)2-based electrode. The specific capacitance values were calculated using the following equation.
C s
=
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
where I is the discharging current, Δt is the discharging time, m is the mass of active material, and ΔV is the potential window. It can be seen from Fig. 5c that the obtained specific capacitance values decrease as the current density increases, which is due to the incremental voltage drop and an insufficient active material involved in the redox reaction at high current density. It also demonstrates that the supercapacitor performance of G10-NH//CC composite electrode among the prepared electrodes is superior to that of NH//CC electrode and due to the incorporation of GQDs (Fig. S4). The calculated specific capacitances of G10-NH//CC are 1825, 1472, 1273, 1113, 988, and 867 F·g−1 at current densities of 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, and 20 A g−1, respectively. This capacitive performance of the binder free G10-NH//CC electrode is comparable to those of previously reported Ni(OH)2 based-electrodes (Table S1). These high capacitance and good rate capability of G10-NH//CC composite suggest that it can be a promising electrode for supercapacitor applications. The electrochemical kinetics were further evaluated by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. Fig. 5d show the Nyquist plots of G10-NH//CC and NH//CC electrodes composites with an equivalent circuit, where Rs represents the ionic resistance of the electrolyte solution, and Rct is charge-transfer resistance at the contact interface between electrode and electrolyte. The plots of both electrodes are composed of a semicircle at the high frequency region and a straight line at the low frequency region. The diameter of semicircle denotes the charge-transfer resistance (Rct), and the G10-NH//CC electrode exhibits a much lower Rct value (1.66 Ω) than the NH//CC electrode (5.55 Ω), indicating that the GQDs enhance the electronic conductivity of electrode.

The cycle stability of the G10-NH//CC electrode was further evaluated by the consecutive CV test up to 8000 cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the specific capacitance increases about 10 % during the first 200 cycles, which is due to the activation of the active materials through a slow diffusion of the electrolyte into nanostructure of G10-NH//CC electrode during the initial cycles. In particular, electrolyte has infiltrated into the nanostructured Ni(OH)2 and the trapped OH- ions gradually diffuse out, resulting in pseudocapacitive performance. [26–28]. It can be determined that 83.5 % of the initial capacitance of G10-NH//CC electrode is retained after a successive 8000 cycles, however the NH//CC electrode retains only 55.2 % of its initial capacitance, indicating the stable cycling performance of the G10-NH//CC electrode. The SEM images of G10-NH//CC electrodes after 2000 and 8000 cycles are shown in Fig. S5. It reveals that G10-NH//CC electrode still maintains its initial microstructure without obvious structural change, which is responsible to the stable cycling performance. In addition, Nyquist plots after 2000 cycles (Fig. 6b) show that Rct of the G10-NH//CC electrode increases slightly to 2.97 Ω and maintains its excellent charge transfer process after the cycle test, compared to the NH//CC electrode (10.01 Ω).

In order to understand the enhanced electrochemical performance by the incorporation of GQDs, the energy band structure of Ni(OH)2 and GQDs was investigated, as shown in Fig. 7 . The bandgap and work function of semiconductor Ni(OH)2 are 2.9 and 3.7 eV, respectively [29]. The energy bandgap and work function of GQDs is related with their size [30], and the size of GQDs in G-NH//CC is about 7 nm. Thus, the bandgap and work function of GQDs is 0.2 and 4.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 7a). When Ni(OH)2 contacts with GQDs, the free electrons will transfer from Ni(O)2 to GQDs at the contact interface and the energy band of Ni(OH)2 bends downward to align the Fermi levels (Fig. 7b), due to higher work function of Ni(OH)2 than GQDs. Therefore, GQDs/Ni(OH)2 forms ohmic contact at the interface, and GQDs are negatively charged while Ni(OH)2 is positively charged. This can facilitate the electrostatic attraction of GQDs with potassium ions of electrolytes as show in Fig. 7b, resulting in enhancing specific capacitance. Moreover, the ohmic contact between GQDs and Ni(OH)2 can improve the electrical conductivity of G-NH//CC electrode.

To further evaluate the practical application of the G10-NH//CC electrode, an all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor (SSC) was assembled. In symmetric device configuration, G10-NH//CC electrodes were used as both the cathode and anode with poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA)/KOH gel electrolyte and separator (Fig. 8 a). The photo images of Fig. 8a show the bending state of the fabricated SSC, implying that the G10-NH//CC electrodes are highly flexible. The CV curves of the fabricated symmetric device at different scan rates were measured in the potential window of 0–1.54 V as shown in Fig. 8b. The CV curves exhibit distorted quasi-rectangular shapes even at a high scan rate without distinct redox peaks, which indicates that the fabricated SSC using G10-NH//CC electrodes performs typical EDLC behavior, suggesting the high rate capability and low internal resistance. Furthermore, Fig. 8c shows the galvanostatic charge-discharge curves of the fabricated SSC at different current densities from 0.5 to 4 A g−1. The specific capacitances of SSC calculated from the curves are 90.9, 80.5, 62.3, and 36.4 F g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1, 2 and 4 A g−1, respectively. The energy density and power density of our SSC was compared with other Ni-based symmetric supercapacitors in the Ragone plot (Fig. 8d). The SSC using G10-NH//CC electrodes delivers an energy density of 80.8 and 32.2 Wh·kg−1 at a power density of 2021 and 16,632 W kg−1, respectively, superior to the previously reported devices [31–37]. To further investigate the flexibility of our SSC, the CV curves were measured under different bending angles at a scan rate of 200 mV s−1 (Fig. 8e). It can be observed that the CV shapes were well maintained even at a bending angle of 180°, suggesting good flexibility and mechanical stability of our SSC. To demonstrate the practical application of G10-NH//CC SSC device, two SSC devices were assembled in series and powered a green light-emitting diode (LED) bulb as shown in Fig. 8f.

In summary, a simple strategy was demonstrated to fabricate the GQDs/Ni(OH)2 nanocomposite on flexible CC via the hydrothermal process. GQDs well embedded in Ni(OH)2 nanowalls can enhance the overall electrical conductivity and ion diffusion, which result in the superior electrochemical performances. Therefore, the obtained G10-NH//CC electrode delivers an excellent specific capacitance of 1825 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and a good cycle stability of 83.5 % capacity retention after 8000 cycles. Moreover, the symmetric supercapacitor using G10-NH//CC electrodes performs a high energy density of 80.8 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 2021 W kg−1 with good flexibility. These results suggest that this novel strategy for fabricating various GQDs/metal oxide or GQDs/metal hydroxide provides great potential for advanced electrochemical energy storage devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by The Ministry of Education (NRF-2018R1D1A1B07047042).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmst.2020.03.063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is supplementary data to this article:

Chen, Ternary hybrids of amorphous nickel hydroxide-carbon nanotube-conducting polymer for supercapacitors with high energy density, excellent rate capability, and long cycle life, Adv. Funct. Mater. 25 (2015) 1063-1073.

J. Yan, T. Wei, W.M. Qiao, B. Shao, Q.K. Zhao, L.J. Zhang, Z.J. Fan, Rapid microwave-assisted synthesis of graphene nanosheet/Co3O4 composite for supercapacitors, Electrochim. Acta 55 (2010) 6973-6978.

L. Mao, C. Guan, X.L. Huang, Q.Q. Ke, Y. Zhang, J. Wang, 3D Graphene-nickel hydroxide hydrogel electrode for high-performance supercapacitor, Electrochim. Acta 196 (2016) 653-660.
